title: Using ucminfcpp from C++
text: C++ core ucminfcpp header-library can embedded directly C++17 project without R Rcpp. article shows C++ developers integrate use optimizer.

title: Using ucminfcpp from C++
text: installing R package, headers available inst/include/ package source tree. can also copy directly GitHub repository: Add directory containing headers compiler’s include path, e.g.:
code: src/include/ucminf_core.hpp src/include/ucminf_core_impl.hpp g++ -std=c++17 -I path/to/ucminfcpp/src/include my_program.cpp -o my_program

title: Using ucminfcpp from C++
text: optimizer returns ucminfcpp::Result struct:
code: namespace ucminfcpp {   struct Result {     std::vector<double> par;   // optimal point     double              value; // f(par)     int                 convergence; // 1 = gradient small, 2 = step small,                                      // 3 = max evals, negative = error     int                 neval; // number of function evaluations   }; }

title: Using ucminfcpp from C++
text: Use minimize() callable type known compile time (example, stored std::function).

title: Using ucminfcpp from C++
text: callable type known compile time, use minimize_direct compiler can inline objective gradient optimizer loop. also works lambdas stateless function objects:
code: #include "ucminf_core.hpp"  // Free function void rosenbrock(const std::vector<double>& x,                 std::vector<double>& g, double& f) {     double a = 1.0 - x[0];     double b = x[1] - x[0] * x[0];     f    =  a * a + 100.0 * b * b;     g[0] = -2.0 * a - 400.0 * x[0] * b;     g[1] =  200.0 * b; }  int main() {     ucminfcpp::Result res = ucminfcpp::minimize_direct({-1.2, 1.0}, rosenbrock);     // ...     return 0; } auto sphere = [](const std::vector<double>& x,                  std::vector<double>& g, double& f) {     f = 0.0;     for (std::size_t i = 0; i < x.size(); ++i) {         f    += x[i] * x[i];         g[i]  = 2.0 * x[i];     } };  ucminfcpp::Result res = ucminfcpp::minimize_direct(     std::vector<double>(10, 2.0), sphere);

title: Using ucminfcpp from C++
text: Pass ucminfcpp::Options struct either API control convergence criteria iteration limits:
code: ucminfcpp::Options opts; opts.maxeval = 2000;   // maximum function evaluations opts.eps     = 1e-10;  // gradient tolerance  ucminfcpp::Result res = ucminfcpp::minimize({-1.2, 1.0}, banana, opts);

title: Using ucminfcpp from C++
text: repository ships CMakeLists.txt builds C++ tests optional Python/Julia bindings. build core tests: embed ucminfcpp CMake project, add src/include directory interface include:
code: cmake -S . -B build -DBUILD_TESTS=ON -DBUILD_PYTHON=OFF -DBUILD_JULIA=OFF cmake --build build ctest --test-dir build target_include_directories(my_target PRIVATE path/to/ucminfcpp/src/include)

what: Example: Integrating into an Existing Optimizer
title: Using ucminfcpp from C++
text: following pattern common scientific computing ucminfcpp replaces numerical optimization dependency: keeps optimizer decoupled specific model, making code easy test extend.
code: #include "ucminf_core.hpp" #include <vector> #include <functional>  // Generic fitting routine ucminfcpp::Result fit_model(     std::vector<double> theta0,     std::function<void(const std::vector<double>&,                        std::vector<double>&, double&)> neg_log_lik) {     return ucminfcpp::minimize(theta0, neg_log_lik); }

title: Introduction to ucminfcpp
text: ucminfcpp R package provides unconstrained nonlinear optimization modern C++17 reimplementation classic Fortran-based ucminf algorithm. drop-replacement ucminf::ucminf(). package primarily developed power nicher xsdmMle packages, fast reliable unconstrained optimization central fitting ecological niche models maximum-likelihood estimators.

title: Introduction to ucminfcpp
text: Banana Function classic benchmark optimization algorithms. global minimum (1, 1).
code: banana <- function(x) {   100 * (x[2] - x[1]^2)^2 + (1 - x[1])^2 }  result <- ucminfcpp::ucminf(c(-1.2, 1), banana) print(result$par)   # should be c(1, 1) #> [1] 0.9996971 0.9993937

title: Introduction to ucminfcpp
text: simple quadratic known minimum (3, -5).
code: quadratic <- function(x) {   (x[1] - 3)^2 + (x[2] + 5)^2 }  result_q <- ucminfcpp::ucminf(c(0, 0), quadratic) print(result_q$par)   # should be c(3, -5) #> [1]  3.000000 -5.000002

title: Introduction to ucminfcpp
text: smooth, differentiable objective can passed ucminfcpp::ucminf().
code: custom_fn <- function(x) {   sum(x^2 - log(1 + x^2)) }  result_c <- ucminfcpp::ucminf(c(1, 1, 1), custom_fn) print(result_c$par)   # should be close to c(0, 0, 0) #> [1] 0.00602089 0.00602089 0.00602089

title: Introduction to ucminfcpp
text: ucminfcpp designed numerically equivalent ucminf. following code confirms roots match Banana Function.
code:
starting_point <- c(-1.2, 1)  result_cpp     <- ucminfcpp::ucminf(starting_point, banana) result_fortran <- ucminf::ucminf(starting_point, banana)  cat("ucminfcpp result:
");  print(result_cpp$par) #> ucminfcpp result: #> [1] 0.9996971 0.9993937 cat("ucminf result:
");     print(result_fortran$par) #> ucminf result: #> [1] 0.9996971 0.9993937  identical_result <- all.equal(result_cpp$par, result_fortran$par) cat("Are the results the same?", identical_result, "
") #> Are the results the same? TRUE

title: Introduction to ucminfcpp
text: Even calling pure R objective functions (cross interpreter boundary every evaluation), ucminfcpp typically outperforms Fortran-based ucminf due reduced C/Fortran call overhead C++ core. benchmark shows median execution time implementation across 100 runs. ucminfcpp achieves comparable better performance maintaining full numerical equivalence. even greater speed, implement objective function C++ call via ucminfcpp::minimize_direct<F>() eliminate interpreter overhead.
code: library(microbenchmark)  benchmark_results <- microbenchmark(   ucminfcpp = ucminfcpp::ucminf(c(-1.2, 1), banana),   ucminf    = ucminf::ucminf(c(-1.2, 1), banana),   times = 100L ) print(benchmark_results) #> Unit: microseconds #>       expr     min      lq      mean  median       uq     max neval #>  ucminfcpp  90.890  93.244  98.29874  95.769  98.3385 154.398   100 #>     ucminf 126.878 129.251 134.58590 131.946 134.9270 180.617   100

title: Using ucminfcpp from Julia
text: ucminfcpp provides Julia bindings via CxxWrap.jl, located julia/ directory source tree. Julia users can call C++ optimizer core directly Julia code, achieving performance par native Julia optimization libraries.

title: Using ucminfcpp from Julia
text: Julia ≥ 1.6 C++17-capable compiler (GCC ≥ 7, Clang ≥ 5) CMake ≥ 3.15 CxxWrap.jl Julia package Install CxxWrap.jl Julia REPL:
code: using Pkg Pkg.add("CxxWrap")

title: Using ucminfcpp from Julia
text: Clone repository build Julia shared library: shared library (e.g. libucminfcpp_julia.) placed build/ directory.
code: git clone https://github.com/alrobles/ucminfcpp.git cd ucminfcpp  cmake -S . -B build \       -DBUILD_JULIA=ON \       -DBUILD_PYTHON=OFF \       -DBUILD_TESTS=OFF  cmake --build build

title: Using ucminfcpp from Julia
code: using CxxWrap  # Load the compiled library @wrapmodule(() -> joinpath(@__DIR__, "build", "libucminfcpp_julia"))  @initcxx

title: Using ucminfcpp from Julia
text: Define objective function Julia function fills gradient vector -place returns function value, call minimize:
code: function banana(x::Vector{Float64}, g::Vector{Float64})     f    = 100.0 * (x[2] - x[1]^2)^2 + (1.0 - x[1])^2     g[1] = -400.0 * x[1] * (x[2] - x[1]^2) - 2.0 * (1.0 - x[1])     g[2] =  200.0 * (x[2] - x[1]^2)     return f end  x0     = [-1.2, 1.0] result = minimize(x0, banana)  println("par  = ", result.par)         # [1.0, 1.0] println("f    = ", result.value)       # ~0.0 println("conv = ", result.convergence)

title: Using ucminfcpp from Julia
code: function quadratic(x::Vector{Float64}, g::Vector{Float64})     f    = (x[1] - 3.0)^2 + (x[2] + 5.0)^2     g[1] = 2.0 * (x[1] - 3.0)     g[2] = 2.0 * (x[2] + 5.0)     return f end  result = minimize([0.0, 0.0], quadratic) println("Minimum at x = ", result.par)   # [3.0, -5.0]

title: Using ucminfcpp from Julia
text: deriving gradient analytically inconvenient, use FiniteDiff.jl:
code: using FiniteDiff  function rosenbrock_scalar(x::Vector{Float64})     return 100.0 * (x[2] - x[1]^2)^2 + (1.0 - x[1])^2 end  function rosenbrock_with_grad(x::Vector{Float64}, g::Vector{Float64})     FiniteDiff.finite_difference_gradient!(g, rosenbrock_scalar, x)     return rosenbrock_scalar(x) end  result = minimize([-1.2, 1.0], rosenbrock_with_grad) println(result.par)   # close to [1.0, 1.0]

title: Using ucminfcpp from Julia
text: ucminfcpp complements native Julia optimizers like Optim.jl: Use ucminfcpp Julia need results numerically identical obtained R Fortran ucminf packages — example, validating Julia model R reference implementation.

title: Using ucminfcpp from Julia
code: function sphere(x::Vector{Float64}, g::Vector{Float64})     f = 0.0     for i in eachindex(x)         f    += x[i]^2         g[i]  = 2.0 * x[i]     end     return f end  x0     = fill(2.0, 20) result = minimize(x0, sphere) println("f(x*) ≈ ", result.value)   # ~0.0

title: Portability from Fortran to C++
text: UCMINF optimization algorithm originally written Fortran 77 Hans Bruun Nielsen Department Mathematical Modelling, Technical University Denmark (DTU), 2000. implements quasi-Newton method : BFGS (Broyden–Fletcher–Goldfarb–Shanno) inverse-Hessian updating, soft line search satisfies Wolfe conditions, adaptive trust-region radius safeguard steps large. original report : Nielsen, H. B. (2000). UCMINF — Algorithm Unconstrained, Nonlinear Optimization. Report IMM-REP-2000-19, DTU. http://www.imm.dtu.dk/documents/ftp/tr00/tr19_00.pdf algorithm later wrapped R Soren Hauberg Claus Ekstrøm ucminf CRAN package, calls original Fortran code via .Fortran().

title: Portability from Fortran to C++
text: Fortran wrapper approach several practical limitations motivated creation ucminfcpp:

title: Portability from Fortran to C++
text: ucminfcpp rewrites core algorithm header-C++17 library located src/include/: algorithm logic faithfully translated line--line Fortran source, preserving convergence criteria, step-size control, gradient tolerances. result numerical equivalence original implementation.
code: src/   include/     ucminf_core.hpp        ← public API declarations     ucminf_core_impl.hpp   ← template implementation (included by core.hpp)   ucminf_core.c            ← low-level numeric helpers (shared with Fortran logic)   ucminf_rcpp.cpp          ← Rcpp wrapper

title: Portability from Fortran to C++
text: Fortran 77 code frequently uses COMMON blocks shared mutable state. C++ rewrite encapsulates optimizer state inside single struct passed reference call stack, eliminating global state making optimizer thread-safe concurrent optimization runs.

title: Portability from Fortran to C++
text: used R, objective function R closure must called Rcpp. design isolates boundary src/ucminf_rcpp.cpp, keeping C++ core (ucminf_core.hpp) free R dependency — depends C++ standard library.

title: Portability from Fortran to C++
text: Fortran line search allocated temporary arrays call. C++ version uses std::vector iterate x gradient g, reuses buffers throughout optimization loop, reducing heap pressure.

what: 4. Dual API for Different Use Cases
title: Portability from Fortran to C++
text: Two public entry points share kernel implementation: lets downstream users choose convenience maximum speed.
code: // High-level: accepts any callable via std::function (slight overhead) ucminf::Result res = ucminf::minimize(x0, fn);  // Zero-overhead: compiler can inline the callable ucminf::Result res = ucminf::minimize_direct<decltype(fn)>(x0, fn);

title: Portability from Fortran to C++
text: test suite tests/cpp/ uses Catch2 verify C++ optimizer matches Fortran results several benchmark functions (Rosenbrock, Himmelblau, sphere, etc.) within machine epsilon.
code: banana <- function(x) 100 * (x[2] - x[1]^2)^2 + (1 - x[1])^2  r_cpp     <- ucminfcpp::ucminf(c(-1.2, 1), banana) r_fortran <- ucminf::ucminf(c(-1.2, 1), banana)  stopifnot(isTRUE(all.equal(r_cpp$par, r_fortran$par))) cat("Results are numerically identical.\n") #> Results are numerically identical.

title: Using ucminfcpp from Python
text: ucminfcpp ships pybind11-based Python extension module located python/ directory source tree. allows Python developers use high-performance C++ optimizer core without going R .

title: Using ucminfcpp from Python
text: Python ≥ 3.8 C++17-capable compiler (GCC ≥ 7, Clang ≥ 5, MSVC 2019+) CMake ≥ 3.15 pybind11 (pip install pybind11)

title: Using ucminfcpp from Python
text: Clone repository build Python extension: successful build shared library (e.g. ucminfcpp.Linux, ucminfcpp.pyd Windows) placed build/ directory. Add PYTHONPATH copy project:
code: git clone https://github.com/alrobles/ucminfcpp.git cd ucminfcpp  cmake -S . -B build \       -DBUILD_PYTHON=ON \       -DBUILD_JULIA=OFF \       -DBUILD_TESTS=OFF  cmake --build build export PYTHONPATH="$PYTHONPATH:$(pwd)/build"

title: Using ucminfcpp from Python
text: Import module call minimize() initial point callable computes function value gradient simultaneously:
code: import ucminfcpp  def banana(x, g):     """Rosenbrock's Banana Function with in-place gradient."""     f       = 100.0 * (x[1] - x[0]**2)**2 + (1.0 - x[0])**2     g[0]    = -400.0 * x[0] * (x[1] - x[0]**2) - 2.0 * (1.0 - x[0])     g[1]    =  200.0 * (x[1] - x[0]**2)     return f  result = ucminfcpp.minimize([-1.2, 1.0], banana) print("par  =", result.par)        # [1.0, 1.0] print("f    =", result.value)      # ~0.0 print("conv =", result.convergence)

title: Using ucminfcpp from Python
code: import ucminfcpp  def quadratic(x, g):     # f(x) = (x0 - 3)^2 + (x1 + 5)^2     f    = (x[0] - 3.0)**2 + (x[1] + 5.0)**2     g[0] = 2.0 * (x[0] - 3.0)     g[1] = 2.0 * (x[1] + 5.0)     return f  x0     = [0.0, 0.0] result = ucminfcpp.minimize(x0, quadratic)  print(f"Minimum at x = {result.par}")   # [3.0, -5.0] print(f"f(x*)       = {result.value}")  # 0.0

what: Using Finite Differences for the Gradient
title: Using ucminfcpp from Python
text: providing analytical gradient inconvenient, can approximate numerically using scipy.optimize.approx_fprime wrap result:
code: import ucminfcpp from scipy.optimize import approx_fprime import numpy as np  def rosenbrock(x):     return 100.0 * (x[1] - x[0]**2)**2 + (1.0 - x[0])**2  eps = np.sqrt(np.finfo(float).eps)  def rosenbrock_with_grad(x, g):     grad = approx_fprime(x, rosenbrock, eps)     for i, gi in enumerate(grad):         g[i] = gi     return rosenbrock(x)  result = ucminfcpp.minimize([-1.2, 1.0], rosenbrock_with_grad) print(result.par)   # close to [1.0, 1.0]

title: Using ucminfcpp from Python
text: ucminfcpp strong alternative scipy.optimize.minimize "BFGS" method smooth, unconstrained problems:

title: Using ucminfcpp from Python
text: Pass keyword arguments adjust optimizer settings:
code: result = ucminfcpp.minimize(     [-1.2, 1.0],     banana,     maxeval = 1000,     eps     = 1e-10 )

title: Key Features of ucminfcpp
text: ucminfcpp solves unconstrained nonlinear optimization problems form: underlying algorithm quasi-Newton method BFGS updating inverse Hessian soft line search adaptive trust-region radius. constraints x imposed; need bounds, consider wrapping problem barrier penalty function.
code: minimise  f(x),   x ∈ ℝⁿ

title: Key Features of ucminfcpp
text: Himmelblau’s function four local minima. Starting different initial points illustrates optimizer converges nearest minimum.

title: Key Features of ucminfcpp
text: ucminfcpp handles objective functions arbitrary dimension n. requirement function accepts numeric vector length n returns scalar.

title: Key Features of ucminfcpp
code: sphere <- function(x) sum(x^2)  x0  <- rep(2, 10) res <- ucminfcpp::ucminf(x0, sphere) cat("Minimum:", res$value, "\n") #> Minimum: 3.131017e-16 cat("Par (first 5):", round(res$par[1:5], 8), "\n") #> Par (first 5): -1e-08 -1e-08 -1e-08 -1e-08 -1e-08

title: Key Features of ucminfcpp
text: Rastrigin function highly multimodal, starting near origin still converges global minimum.
code: rastrigin <- function(x) {   n <- length(x)   10 * n + sum(x^2 - 10 * cos(2 * pi * x)) }  res_r <- ucminfcpp::ucminf(rep(0.1, 5), rastrigin) cat("Minimum:", res_r$value, "\n") #> Minimum: 1.421085e-14 cat("Par:", round(res_r$par, 6), "\n") #> Par: 0 0 0 0 0

title: Key Features of ucminfcpp
text: Providing exact gradient via gr argument speeds convergence improves accuracy, finite-difference approximation skipped.
code: banana      <- function(x) 100 * (x[2] - x[1]^2)^2 + (1 - x[1])^2 banana_grad <- function(x) {   c(-400 * x[1] * (x[2] - x[1]^2) - 2 * (1 - x[1]),      200 * (x[2] - x[1]^2)) }  res_g <- ucminfcpp::ucminf(c(-1.2, 1), banana, gr = banana_grad) cat("par:", res_g$par, "\n") #> par: 1 1 cat("convergence:", res_g$convergence, "\n") #> convergence: 1

title: Key Features of ucminfcpp
text: Starting exactly minimum return immediately without degrading result.
code: quadratic <- function(x) (x[1] - 3)^2 + (x[2] + 5)^2 res_min   <- ucminfcpp::ucminf(c(3, -5), quadratic) cat("f(3,-5) =", res_min$value, "\n")   # should be ~0 #> f(3,-5) = 0

title: Key Features of ucminfcpp
text: Functions broad, nearly flat minimum handled gracefully; optimizer stops gradient norm falls tolerance.
code: flat_fn <- function(x) {   exp(-0.01 * sum(x^2))   # very flat; minimum spread over a large area }  # Negate to turn maximum into minimum res_flat <- ucminfcpp::ucminf(c(5, 5), function(x) -flat_fn(x)) cat("convergence code:", res_flat$convergence, "\n") #> convergence code: 1

title: Key Features of ucminfcpp
text: tuning parameters original ucminf supported:
code: res_ctrl <- ucminfcpp::ucminf(   c(-1.2, 1), banana,   control = list(maxeval = 1000, trace = 0) ) cat("evaluations:", res_ctrl$info["neval"], "\n") #> evaluations: 42

what: C++ Reimplementation of the ucminf Unconstrained Nonlinear Optimizer
title: C++ Reimplementation of the ucminf Unconstrained Nonlinear Optimizer
text: R package provides unconstrained nonlinear optimization modern C++17 reimplementation classic Fortran-based ucminf algorithm. drop-replacement ucminf::ucminf(). reimplementation C++ library offers multi-language support Python Julia .

title: C++ Reimplementation of the ucminf Unconstrained Nonlinear Optimizer
text: Drop-replacement original ucminf::ucminf() function. Modern C++17 implementation, enabling easier extension integration. Multi-language support – use algorithm R, Python, Julia. Efficient robust – retains numerical properties original Fortran code. Easy installation – Fortran compiler required.

title: C++ Reimplementation of the ucminf Unconstrained Nonlinear Optimizer
text: Install latest development version GitHub:
code: # Install directly from GitHub devtools::install_github("alrobles/ucminfcpp")

title: C++ Reimplementation of the ucminf Unconstrained Nonlinear Optimizer
text: example optimises Rosenbrock’s Banana Function confirms ucminfcpp ucminf produce identical results.
code:
# Rosenbrock's Banana Function banana <- function(x) {   100 * (x[2] - x[1]^2)^2 + (1 - x[1])^2 }  # Initial point starting_point <- c(-1.2, 1)  # Optimization with ucminfcpp result_cpp <- ucminfcpp::ucminf(starting_point, banana) cat("ucminfcpp result:
") print(result_cpp$par)  # Optimization with ucminf result_fortran <- ucminf::ucminf(starting_point, banana) cat("ucminf result:
") print(result_fortran$par)  # Check similarity identical_result <- all.equal(result_cpp$par, result_fortran$par) cat("Are the results the same? ", identical_result, "
")

title: C++ Reimplementation of the ucminf Unconstrained Nonlinear Optimizer
text: ucminfcpp provides ucminf() function general-purpose unconstrained nonlinear optimization. algorithm quasi-Newton method BFGS updating inverse Hessian soft line search adaptive trust-region radius monitoring. designed drop-replacement original Fortran-based ucminf ucminf::ucminf() function. original algorithm written Fortran Hans Bruun Nielsen.

(2000) UCMINF - Algorithm Unconstrained, Nonlinear Optimization, Report IMM-REP-2000-19, Department Mathematical Modelling, Technical University Denmark. can consult document , original Fortran source code archived . title: General-Purpose Unconstrained Non-Linear Optimization (C/Rcpp) — ucminf
text: implementation UCMINF algorithm translated Fortran C wrapped Rcpp. algorithm uses quasi-Newton method BFGS updating inverse Hessian soft line search trust-region radius monitoring.

title: General-Purpose Unconstrained Non-Linear Optimization (C/Rcpp) — ucminf
code: ucminf(   par,   fn = NULL,   gr = NULL,   ...,   fdfun = NULL,   control = list(),   hessian = 0 )

title: General-Purpose Unconstrained Non-Linear Optimization (C/Rcpp) — ucminf
text: par Initial estimate minimum (numeric vector). fn Objective function minimized. Must return scalar. Ignored fdfun supplied. gr Gradient function. NULL finite-difference approximation used. Ignored fdfun supplied. ... Optional arguments passed fn gr. fdfun Optional combined value+gradient function fdfun(x) returning list(f = scalar, g = numeric_vector). supplied, fn gr ignored. control list control parameters (see Details), ucminf_control object. hessian Integer controlling Hessian output: 0 Hessian (default). 2 Returns final inverse-Hessian approximation BFGS. 3 Returns inverse Hessian (2) inverse (Hessian).

title: General-Purpose Unconstrained Non-Linear Optimization (C/Rcpp) — ucminf
text: list class "ucminf" elements: par Computed minimizer. value Objective value minimizer. convergence Termination code: 1 Small gradient (grtol). 2 Small step (xtol). 3 Evaluation limit (maxeval). 4 Zero step line search. -2 n <= 0. -4 stepmax <= 0. -5 grtol xtol <= 0. -6 maxeval <= 0. -7 Given inverse Hessian positive definite. message Human-readable termination message. invhessian.lt Lower triangle final inverse-Hessian   approximation (packed). invhessian Full inverse-Hessian matrix (hessian >= 2). hessian Hessian matrix, inverse invhessian   (hessian == 3). info Named vector: maxgradient \(\|g(x)\|_\infty\) solution. laststep Length last step. stepmax Final trust-region radius. neval Number function/gradient evaluations.

title: General-Purpose Unconstrained Non-Linear Optimization (C/Rcpp) — ucminf
text: package two-phase migration original ucminf R package: Phase 1: Fortran core translated C         (src/ucminf_core.c). Phase 2: C core wrapped Rcpp interface         (src/ucminf_rcpp.cpp). interface designed interchangeable original ucminf::ucminf optim. control argument accepts: trace positive, print convergence info optimization.     Default 0. grtol Stop \(\|g(x)\|_\infty \le\) grtol.     Default 1e-6. xtol Stop \(\|x - x_{\rm prev}\|^2 \le\)     xtol*(xtol + \(\|x\|^2\)). Default 1e-12. stepmax Initial trust-region radius. Default 1. maxeval Maximum function evaluations. Default 500. grad Finite-difference type gr = NULL:     "forward" (default) "central". gradstep Length-2 vector; step     \(|x_i| \cdot \code{gradstep[1]} + \code{gradstep[2]}\).     Default c(1e-6, 1e-8). invhessian.lt vector containing lower triangle     initial inverse Hessian (packed column-major). Default: identity.

title: General-Purpose Unconstrained Non-Linear Optimization (C/Rcpp) — ucminf
text: Nielsen, H. B. (2000). UCMINF – Algorithm Unconstrained, Nonlinear Optimization. Report IMM-REP-2000-19, DTU.

title: General-Purpose Unconstrained Non-Linear Optimization (C/Rcpp) — ucminf
code: ## Rosenbrock Banana function fR <- function(x) (1 - x[1])^2 + 100 * (x[2] - x[1]^2)^2 gR <- function(x) c(-400 * x[1] * (x[2] - x[1] * x[1]) - 2 * (1 - x[1]),                     200 * (x[2] - x[1] * x[1]))  ## Find minimum with analytic gradient ucminf(par = c(2, 0.5), fn = fR, gr = gR) #> ucminf result #>   Converged: Stopped by small gradient (grtol).  #>   Minimum value: 8.073e-20  #>   Minimizer: #> [1] 1 1 #>   Evaluations: 21  #>   Max |gradient|: 1.021e-08   ## Find minimum with finite-difference gradient ucminf(par = c(2, 0.5), fn = fR) #> ucminf result #>   Converged: Stopped by zero step from line search.  #>   Minimum value: 9.007e-08  #>   Minimizer: #> [1] 0.9997 0.9994 #>   Evaluations: 24  #>   Max |gradient|: 8.065e-05

what: Build a validated control list for ucminf() — ucminf_control
title: Build a validated control list for ucminf() — ucminf_control
text: Returns named list control parameters can passed directly ucminf(), ucminf_xptr(), etc.

title: Build a validated control list for ucminf() — ucminf_control
code: ucminf_control(   trace = 0,   grtol = 1e-06,   xtol = 1e-12,   stepmax = 1,   maxeval = 500L,   grad = c("forward", "central"),   gradstep = c(1e-06, 1e-08),   invhessian.lt = NULL )

title: Build a validated control list for ucminf() — ucminf_control
text: trace Integer. > 0, print convergence info. Default 0. grtol Gradient tolerance. Default 1e-6. xtol Step tolerance. Default 1e-12. stepmax Initial trust-region radius. Default 1. maxeval Maximum function evaluations. Default 500. grad Finite-difference type: "forward" "central". Default "forward". gradstep Length-2 step vector. Default c(1e-6, 1e-8). invhessian.lt Packed lower-triangle initial inverse Hessian. Default NULL.

title: Build a validated control list for ucminf() — ucminf_control
text: list class "ucminf_control".

title: Unconstrained Nonlinear Optimization (C++/Rcpp implementation) — ucminf_cpp
text: Internal function called ucminf. Use instead.

title: Unconstrained Nonlinear Optimization (C++/Rcpp implementation) — ucminf_cpp
code: ucminf_cpp(par, fn, gr, has_gr, control)

title: Unconstrained Nonlinear Optimization (C++/Rcpp implementation) — ucminf_cpp
text: par Numeric vector starting values. fn R function returning objective value. gr R function returning gradient, NULL. has_gr Logical: TRUE gr provided. control Named list control parameters: grtol, xtol, stepmax, maxeval, grad_type, gradstep, invhessian_lt.

title: Unconstrained Nonlinear Optimization (C++/Rcpp implementation) — ucminf_cpp
text: list elements par, value, convergence,   neval, maxgradient, laststep, stepmax,   invhessian_lt.

what: Optimize using a compiled C++ objective (XPtr interface) — ucminf_xptr
title: Optimize using a compiled C++ objective (XPtr interface) — ucminf_xptr
text: Calls UCMINF optimizer compiled C++ objective function wrapped Rcpp::XPtr<ucminf::ObjFun>. path bypasses R interpreter every function evaluation, giving maximum performance non-trivial objectives.

title: Optimize using a compiled C++ objective (XPtr interface) — ucminf_xptr
code: ucminf_xptr(par, xptr, control = list(), hessian = 0)

title: Optimize using a compiled C++ objective (XPtr interface) — ucminf_xptr
text: par Numeric starting vector. xptr externalptr created wrapping ucminf::ObjFun* Rcpp::XPtr<ucminf::ObjFun>. control named list control parameters (see ucminf), ucminf_control object. hessian Integer: 0 = none, 2 = inv-Hessian, 3 = .

title: Optimize using a compiled C++ objective (XPtr interface) — ucminf_xptr
text: list class "ucminf" (structure ucminf).
